contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: A variable sum window is justified when extending and shrinking have predictable directional effects, typically under nonnegative values; without that monotonic boundary, pointer movement cannot safely discard candidates.

items.prompt: All values are positive, and the task asks for the minimum-length contiguous interval with sum at least target. Which invariant supports a variable window?

options.text: After right makes the window valid, repeatedly move left while validity remains, recording each shortest valid candidate.
Move left whenever the sum is below target.
Never shrink after reaching target.
Positive values forbid sliding windows.

feedback.reason: With positive values, expansion strictly increases sum and shrinking strictly decreases it.
feedback.details: Once valid, removing left explores shorter candidates until the next removal would break validity.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.shrink_invalid: Removing a positive value cannot repair an insufficient sum.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_only: They provide the monotonicity the window needs.

items.prompt: All values are nonnegative, and the task asks for the longest interval with sum at most budget. Which rule is natural?

options.text: Expand right; while sum exceeds budget, move left; record the valid length afterward.
Shrink while sum is below budget.
Stop at the first valid interval.
Use exact-sum prefix lookup because the objective is at most.

feedback.reason: Expansion cannot decrease the sum, so an over-budget interval remains over budget until enough left values are removed.
feedback.details: After restoring validity, the current interval is the longest ending at right under this process.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.shrink_under_budget: That throws away valid candidates without necessity.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.stop_first_valid: Longer valid intervals may appear later.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.exact_prefix_only: The threshold and nonnegative values give a monotone window.

items.prompt: Why do positive values create a legal discard rule for a minimum-sum-threshold window?

options.text: Any further right extension can only increase sum, and removing left can only decrease it.
All prefix sums become equal.
Indices are ordered in every array.
The target is numerically sorted.

feedback.reason: The sign constraint links each boundary move to a known aggregate direction.
feedback.details: That implication is the proof that skipped shorter or earlier intervals cannot later become better in the forbidden direction.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.indices_monotonic: Index order alone says nothing about sum direction.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.target_sorted: A scalar target has no sort order role.

items.prompt: Zeros are allowed in a nonnegative minimum-length sum-at-least-target problem. Which statement is correct?

options.text: The window remains monotone, but zero removals may leave the sum unchanged, so the loop must still advance boundaries and test shorter candidates.
Any zero invalidates the window method.
Every right expansion strictly increases sum.
Prefix hashing is the only correct strategy.

feedback.reason: Nonnegative values preserve nondecreasing expansion and nonincreasing shrinkage.
feedback.details: Zeros affect strictness, not the directional invariant.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.zero_breaks: Zero does not reverse sum direction.

items.prompt: For positive values, when should a minimum-length sum-at-least-target algorithm record an answer?

options.text: Whenever the current window is valid, before removing its left value, and again while validity continues.
Only after the entire array is processed.
Only when the sum drops below target.
Only the first time any window reaches target.

feedback.reason: Every valid state in the shrink loop is a candidate.
feedback.details: Recording before the next removal preserves the shortest valid interval found at that right boundary.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_end: The best interval may have been valid earlier and then discarded.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_invalid: That state is not a valid answer.

items.prompt: For nonnegative values, an exact-sum-k existence problem is considered. Which conclusion is accurate?

options.text: A carefully defined variable window can work because sum movement is monotone, although zeros and equality handling need explicit rules.
Exact equality always forbids a window.
The same rule works unchanged for arbitrary negative values.
Sort the values and use opposite ends.

feedback.reason: Objective semantics and value constraints jointly decide the method.
feedback.details: Prefix-hash state is more general for arbitrary signs, but exact equality is not itself enough to ban a window under nonnegative values.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_only: Nonnegative values can support an equality window.

items.prompt: The target is zero and all values are strictly positive. What should a non-empty minimum-length sum-at-least-target contract decide?

options.text: Every non-empty interval is valid, so the minimum length is 1 if the array is non-empty.
Return length 0 even though empty intervals are forbidden.
No positive sum can be at least zero.
Only a prefix-frequency map can answer.

feedback.reason: Edge-case behavior depends on whether empty intervals are allowed.
feedback.details: For non-empty intervals and positive values, any singleton satisfies a nonpositive threshold.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.zero_length: The result contract excludes the empty interval.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.no_answer: Every positive sum is at least zero.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_hash: The boundary is trivial under the stated contract.

items.prompt: No interval reaches a positive target in a positive-valued array. What should the window algorithm return?

options.text: The contract's explicit no-solution result after scanning all right boundaries.
The final interval even though it is invalid.
Always zero regardless of the API contract.
The total array sum as a length.

feedback.reason: Monotonicity makes the search complete, not guaranteed to succeed.
feedback.details: The public result contract must define absence explicitly.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.last_window: A result must satisfy the threshold.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_sum_total: Sum and length are different result types.

items.prompt: Which change destroys the positive monotonic boundary most directly?

options.text: Allow arbitrary negative values.
Increase the target while values remain nonnegative.
Increase n.
Use a wider numeric type.

feedback.reason: A negative incoming or outgoing value can reverse the aggregate effect of a boundary move.
feedback.details: That removes the simple discard implication.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.more_elements: Input size affects work, not legality.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.use_long_integer: That can improve overflow safety without changing monotonicity.

items.prompt: Which facts justify a positive or nonnegative threshold window? Select all that apply.

options.text: Adding the next value cannot decrease the sum.
Removing the left value cannot increase the sum.
The candidate is one contiguous interval with movable boundaries.
The prompt contains the word target.

feedback.reason: The method needs both a matching interval search space and directional aggregate effects.
feedback.details: Target vocabulary alone is insufficient.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.target_keyword: A keyword does not establish monotonicity.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.contiguous_objective: The state matches the search space.

items.prompt: Which objectives naturally fit a nonnegative variable window? Select all that apply.

options.text: Minimum length with sum at least target.
Maximum length with sum at most budget.
Existence of exact sum k with carefully handled zeros.
Exact sum k with arbitrary signed values using the same simple rule.

feedback.reason: The objective must align with the direction supplied by nonnegative values.
feedback.details: The same template does not transfer to arbitrary signs.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.max_at_most: Shrink only when invalid and record restored windows.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.exact_nonnegative: Monotone sum movement can support equality.

items.prompt: Which edge cases require explicit loop or result rules? Select all that apply.

options.text: Zeros that leave the sum unchanged while a boundary moves.
No valid interval.
A nonpositive threshold when empty intervals may or may not be legal.
Only one ordinary case with a unique middle answer.

feedback.reason: Monotone correctness does not remove API and loop-boundary decisions.
feedback.details: These cases expose hidden empty-window and progress assumptions.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.positive_middle: This does not cover boundary semantics.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.zeros: They test strict versus non-strict conditions.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.no_solution: The absence sentinel must be defined.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.target_nonpositive: The result depends on the interval contract.

items.prompt: Which mistaken claims bypass the monotonic-window proof? Select all that apply.

options.text: Every contiguous-sum problem supports a simple window.
Using left and right variables proves safe elimination.
Nonnegative values can create monotone sum movement.
The first valid threshold interval is always globally shortest.

feedback.reason: A correct window argument must name the monotone effect and complete answer search.
feedback.details: Nonnegative monotonicity is real evidence, not a heuristic.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.contiguous_always: Value signs and objective semantics matter.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.two_pointers_proof: The directional invariant is the proof.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.first_valid_best: Later right boundaries may produce shorter valid intervals.

items.prompt: Sequence the minimization pass for a positive sum-at-least window.

elements.text: Add a[right] as right advances.
While the sum is at least target, the current window is a candidate.
Record its length against the best answer.
Subtract a[left], increment left, and continue while still valid.

feedback.reason: The shrink loop enumerates all shortest candidates ending at each right boundary.
feedback.details: Recording before removal prevents losing the current valid interval.

items.prompt: Sequence the repair-and-measure pass for a nonnegative sum-at-most window.

elements.text: Add the new right value.
While the sum exceeds budget, subtract left values and advance left.
Once the sum is at most budget, the current window is valid.
Update the maximum length with right - left + 1.

feedback.reason: For nonnegative values, shrinking is the only way to repair an over-budget interval.
feedback.details: The restored interval is the longest valid one ending at the current right under this scan.

items.prompt: Arrange the correctness proof for the shrink step in a minimum-length positive window.

elements.text: The outgoing left value is positive or nonnegative.
Removing it cannot increase the sum.
Each shrink produces a strictly shorter interval at the same right boundary.
When the next state is invalid, further shrinking cannot restore threshold validity.

feedback.reason: The stop rule follows from monotone contraction.
feedback.details: Without the sign condition, an invalid shorter state could become valid after removing a negative value.

items.prompt: Arrange the zero-aware review of a nonnegative window.

elements.text: State that aggregate movement is nondecreasing or nonincreasing, not necessarily strict.
Ensure every loop iteration changes a boundary even when sum stays equal.
Evaluate shorter or longer equal-sum windows according to the objective.
Test leading, trailing, and interior zero runs.

feedback.reason: Zeros preserve legality but expose stalled or prematurely stopped loops.
feedback.details: Boundary progress and answer timing must be tested separately from sum change.

items.prompt: Arrange the decision audit before applying a threshold window.

elements.text: State minimum/maximum and at-least/at-most semantics.
State whether values are positive, nonnegative, or arbitrary signed.
Derive the aggregate effect of expanding and shrinking.
Use the window only if those effects justify every discard and stopping rule.

feedback.reason: The template is derived from objective and value domain.
feedback.details: Contiguity is necessary but not sufficient.

items.prompt: A nonnegative variable-window algorithm advances right across n values and advances left at most n times. Select total time and auxiliary space when only sum and boundaries are stored.
constraints: Each boundary move performs O(1) work.

feedback.reason: The nested while loop is not quadratic because each pointer crosses each index at most once.
feedback.details: This time bound is separate from the monotonicity proof that makes the moves correct.

items.prompt: A brute-force algorithm evaluates every contiguous interval of an n-value array using an O(1) incremental sum per extension. Select worst-case time and auxiliary space.
constraints: The algorithm starts an extension scan from every left boundary.

feedback.reason: There are Θ(n²) contiguous intervals.
feedback.details: The legal monotone window reduces this search only under the stated value and objective constraints.

contrastSets.transferBoundary: A variable sum window is justified when extending and shrinking have predictable directional effects, typically under nonnegative values; without that monotonic boundary, pointer movement cannot safely discard candidates.
